preamble: The voiced palatal lateral approximant /ʎ/, a sound found in languages like Spanish and Italian, can undergo a phonetic transformation known as assibilation, leading to the voiced alveolo-palatal fricative /ʑ/. This shift involves a change in the manner of articulation, where the airflow transitions from a smooth lateral release to a turbulent, central sibilant sound, often accompanied by a slight adjustment in the place of articulation from palatal to alveolo-palatal.
phoneticEffects: Assibilation, fortition, loss of laterality, palatal to alveolo-palatal shift

examples.note: In the history of Spanish, Late Latin /lj/ (realized as [ʎ]) assibilated to a palatal sibilant, which was [ʒ] in Late Old Spanish. This is an instance of the broader shift from /ʎ/ to a voiced alveolo-palatal fricative /ʑ/.

sources: Rutgers Optimality Archive. (2021). The Phonetics and Phonology of Sibilants: A Synchronic and Diachronic OT Typology of Sibilant Inventories.
